coredata.dc:title: Construction of a carbon dots-based Z-scheme photocatalytic electrode with enhanced visible-light-driven activity for Cr(VI) reduction and carbamazepine degradation in different reaction systems
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Jun
Pei, Yuansheng
Hou, Jiaqi
Zheng, Yiming
Zhang, Yu
Wei, Kunhao
He, Xiaosong
Xi, Beidou
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalytic electrode materials commonly exhibit limited degradation of organic pollutants and toxic metal reduction owing to the low separation efficiency of the electron-hole pair. To enhance the photocatalytic activity, a visible-light-driven Z-scheme TiO2-carbon dots-polyaniline (PANI) photocatalytic electrode was fabricated. After co-decorating with carbon dots and PANI, the light adsorption capability was improved, the bandgap was narrowed by 0.4 eV, the photocurrent density increased by 18.8 times under visible-light excitation and 9.5 times at a bias of 0.95 VRHE, resulting in good simultaneous carbamazepine degradation (44.67%) and Cr(VI) reduction (11.94%) performance. Three photoelectrochemical (PEC) systems were then constructed to further strengthen the water purification performance of the photocatalytic electrode. The simultaneous carbamazepine degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performances were further enhanced to 77.63%-83.29% and 23.70%-25.68%, respectively. The good carbamazepine degradation was attributed to the radical process (•OH, 78.63%). Dehydration, hydrolysis, deaminisation, de-ketonisation, and ring-opened reactions were directly responsible for the decomposition and mineralization of CBZ.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic electrode
Z-scheme heterojunction
Carbamazepine
Cr(VI)
Photoelectrochemistry system

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the photocatalytic electrode and cathode
Evaluation of the water purification performances
Evaluation of the water purification performance
Estimation of the stability, the key factors, and the degradation pathways

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis processes of the photocatalytic electrodes.

The top-view field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) images of (a) TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNAs), (b) carbon quantum dots (CQD)-modified TNAs (TC), (c) polyaniline (PANI)-decorated TNAs (TP), and (d) PANI co-decorated TNAs-CQDs-PANI (TCP).
High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the CQDs.

Characterisation of the prepared electrodes. (a) X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns; (b) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra; (c) Ti 2p, (d) C 1 s, (e) N 1 s, (f) O 1 s high-resolution spectra.
Energy band alignment analysis. (a) UV–vis DRS spectra, (b) Tauc plots, (c) Mott–Schottky curves, and (d) valence band XPS spectra.
The transient photocurrent densities of the photocatalytic electrodes in response to variations in the (a) light irradiation; (b) LSV curves.
EIS results. Typical Nyquist plots (a) in dark and (b) under visible-light irradiation and Bode phase angle plots (c) in dark and (d) under visible-light irradiation.
Devolutions of the CBZ and Cr(VI) concentrations in the photocatalytic system. (a) TNAs, (b) TC, (c) TP, and (d) TCP.

EPR spectra using DMPO and the energy band structures of (a) TNAs, (b) TC, (c) TP, and (d) TCP. Note: PA, PB, and PC represent the pathway A (Cr(VI) reduction process), pathway B (direct oxidation CBZ process), and pathway C (indirect oxidation CBZ process), respectively.

Mechanism for the simultaneous CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction of TCP driven by visible light. Ⅰ and Ⅱ are the adsorption processes on the TCP and PVA-rGO surface, respectively; III is the CBZ degradation process by the TCP; Ⅳ and Ⅴ are the Cr(VI) reduction process by the TCP and PVA-rGO, respectively; and VI is the electron transfer process via the external circuit.

Water purification performance stability of TCP in different reaction systems. (a) Variation of the TOC during the degradation stage, (b) TOC removal efficiency, (c) CBZ degradation efficiency, and (d) Cr(VI) reduction efficiency.
Proposed CBZ degradation pathways in different reaction systems.

head.ce:title: Construction of a carbon dots-based Z-scheme photocatalytic electrode with enhanced visible-light-driven activity for Cr(VI) reduction and carbamazepine degradation in different reaction systems

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Environmental Protection Key Laboratory of Simulation and Control of Groundwater Pollution
Chinese Research Academy of Environmental Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic electrode materials commonly exhibit limited degradation of organic pollutants and toxic metal reduction owing to the low separation efficiency of the electron-hole pair. To enhance the photocatalytic activity, a visible-light-driven Z-scheme TiO2-carbon dots-polyaniline (PANI) photocatalytic electrode was fabricated. After co-decorating with carbon dots and PANI, the light adsorption capability was improved, the bandgap was narrowed by 0.4 eV, the photocurrent density increased by 18.8 times under visible-light excitation and 9.5 times at a bias of 0.95 VRHE, resulting in good simultaneous carbamazepine degradation (44.67%) and Cr(VI) reduction (11.94%) performance. Three photoelectrochemical (PEC) systems were then constructed to further strengthen the water purification performance of the photocatalytic electrode. The simultaneous carbamazepine degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performances were further enhanced to 77.63%-83.29% and 23.70%-25.68%, respectively. The good carbamazepine degradation was attributed to the radical process (•OH, 78.63%). Dehydration, hydrolysis, deaminisation, de-ketonisation, and ring-opened reactions were directly responsible for the decomposition and mineralization of CBZ.

ce:section.ce:para: Industrial effluents containing medications, personal care products, and toxic metals, have attracted worldwide attention because they threaten the public health and ecological environment [1]. Hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) has been widely detected in industrial effluents, and its concentration has been strictly limited owing to its high toxicity and solubility [2,3]. Carbamazepine (CBZ) is one of the medications and personal care products that coexist with Cr(VI), forming complex pollutants in water environment. Although the effect of CBZ on the environment is still under investigation, its biotoxicity is a serious threat to public health and the ecological environment [4,5]. Therefore, simultaneous CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction would be of great significance for the improvement of water quality and water environment.

Further research is needed to enhance photocatalytic activity. TiO2 has been widely studied owing to its stability, low cost, and outstanding photosensitivity. [12]. However, wide band gaps (anatase: 3.20 eV; rutile: 3.00 eV) determine the weak light absorption capability, resulting in an unsatisfactory photoelectric conversion performance. Therefore, various materials can be used to modify TiO2 by constructing a heterojunction structure. Among these materials, the delocalised π-π structure materials are well recognised as good candidates owing to the rapid and high separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs, including C3N4, polyaniline (PANI), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [13,14]. For example, previous studies have reported that the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 is significantly improved after the decoration of PANI or CQDs [15–20].

According to the energy band structure, an organic–inorganic hybridisation type Ⅱ heterojunction structure is formed after decorating PANI on the TiO2 surface, resulting in good photocatalytic activity. However, the ECB of TiO2 is slightly lower than that of O2/•O2 –, and the HOMO (PANI) is lower than that of H2O/•OH, which can be solved by constructing a Z-scheme heterojunction structure. Therefore, CQDs with high electroconductivity are introduced as the electron transfer medium, forming TiO2-CQDs-PANI with an indirect Z-scheme structure [21]. However, CQDs are highly soluble in water and are absorbed on the surface of TNAs driven by Van der Waals forces, resulting in a weak binding capability [22]. Therefore, stability is crucial for the efficient photocatalytic activity of TiO2-CQDs-PANI.

The reaction system determines the degradation capability of the photocatalytic material. Compared to the photocatalytic system, a photoelectrochemical (PEC) system is constructed by adding a cathode to strengthen the directional electron transfer capability from the photoanode to the cathode [23]. Normally, a PEC system consists of a photoanode with high photocatalytic activity, a cathode with strong conductivity, and an external circuit. The photogenerated electrons are produced at the photoanode under light excitation and transferred to the cathode via the external circuit [24], enhancing the separation of the electron-hole pairs. Therefore, the optimisation of the reaction systems can make full use of the photocatalytic materials.

In the present work, TiO2 nanotube arrays (TNAs), CQD-modified TNAs (TC), PANI-decorated TNAs (TP), and CQDs and PANI co-decorated TNAs-CQDs-PANI (TCP) photoanodes were fabricated. The morphology, crystal structure, chemical composition, and photocatalytic activity of the fabricated photoanodes were characterised. Then, the simultaneous CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performances, key factors, and stability of the fabricated photoanodes in different degradation systems were evaluated. Finally, the degradation intermediates were detected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the photocatalytic electrode and cathode
ce:section.ce:para: Detail information on the materials used can be found in Text S1. Prior to anode oxidation, Ti foil was pre-treated (Text S2). TNAs were prepared via anode oxidation and low-temperature sintering. The pre-treated Ti foil and the Pt foil were used as the anode and cathode, respectively, and the electrolyte was composed of a 97% ethylene glycol solution containing 0.5 wt% NH4F. The potential and time of the pre-oxidation stage were set as 60 V and 10 min, respectively. The pre-oxidised Ti foil was ultrasonically cleaned in ultrapure water for 60 min. Then, the oxidation conditions were set as 40 V for 30 min in the above electrolyte, and the obtained TNAs were annealed at 150 °C for 6 h in air.

TC was fabricated by a dipping process. Prior to dipping, CQDs were synthesised using the acid-assisted bottom-up method (Text S3). TNAs were immersed in a solution containing 10 g/L CQDs and 10 v% 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA). The TC was solidified at 80 °C for 24 h after 48 h of immersion. Both TP and TCP were fabricated by in-situ electro-polymerisation. TNAs and TC were used as working electrodes (WE) for the synthesis of TP and TCP, respectively. Pt and Ag/AgCl were used as counter electrode (CE) and reference electrode (RE), respectively. An acetone solution containing 0.2 M aniline and 0.05 M citric acid was used as the polymerisation electrolyte, which was aerated with N2 over 30 min to deoxidise. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was applied to aggregate aniline using an electrochemical workstation (PGSTAT204, Metrohm, Switzerland) from 0 V to 0.8 V over 10 cycles (Fig. S1). The obtained TP and TCP were solidified at 60 °C for 24 h. The synthesis processes of the photocatalytic electrode are shown in Fig. 1 .

In this work, we tested the simultaneous CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performance using photocatalysis and PEC systems. Therefore, a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)-reinforced rGO aerogel cathode was synthesised using the hydrothermal method. GO powder was synthesised using a modified Hummers’ method (Text S4). Then, 2 g of GO powder and 0.2 g of PVA were blended and dissolved in 150 mL ultrapure water. After 30 min of ultrasonic dispersion, the mixed solution was heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Then, the obtained rGO cathode was placed at −20 °C for 2 h to freeze and at room temperature (23 °C) for 6 h to thaw. After 10 freeze–thaw cycles, an rGO aerogel with good strength was obtained. Optical photos of the prepared electrodes are shown in Fig. S2.

The morphologies of the prepared electrodes before and after the cycle experiment were observed using a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi, Japan), and the CQDs were observed via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA), respectively. The photocatalytic electrode components were measured using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS binding energies were calibrated by referencing the C 1 s peak to 284.6 eV. The valence-band positions of photocatalytic electrodes were evaluated by performing valence-band XPS. The Raman spectra of the cathode were measured. Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR) spectra of the CQDs and PANI were acquired in the region of 800–4000 cm−1. The specific surface area of the prepared photoanodes and the rGO aerogel were measured using the BET protocol on an Autosorb iQ automated gas adsorption analyser (Quantachrome Instruments, USA). The light adsorption capability was evaluated using ultraviolet–visible absorption diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS, Cary 5000, Agilent, USA) from 200 nm to 800 nm. The production of free radicals under visible-light irradiation was identified using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Elexsys E560, Bruker, USA). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F7000, Hitachi, Japan) with an excitation wavelength of 300 nm.

The photocatalytic activity was evaluated using electrochemical methods. The photocurrent density, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott–Schottky curves were measured using an electrochemical workstation (PGSTAT204, Metrohm, Switzerland). Ag/AgCl and Pt foil were used as the RE and CE, respectively. EIS was measured in the frequency range of 10-2-105 Hz after achieving a stable open-circuit potential (EOCP ) in 0.5 M Na2SO4. The photocurrent density signals in response to the intermittent light irradiation and the bias voltage in the range of −0.4–1 V were recorded during on/off light cycles in 0.5 M Na2SO4. Mott–Schottky measurements occurred at a frequency of 1000 Hz with a step of 20 mV in 0.5 M Na2SO4. A 300 W Xe lamp (HSX-F300 NBeT, China) with a 420 nm cut-off filter was used as the visible-light source. Prior to irradiation, the light source was turned on for 15 min to guarantee a stable light intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the water purification performances
ce:section.ce:para: Detail information on the experimental setups and the reaction systems are listed in Tables S1 and S2, respectively. In this work, a traditional photocatalytic system (Fig. S3), a PEC system with a single reactor (Fig. S4), and a PEC system with two reactors (Fig. S5) were used. Before the water purification experiment, all electrodes were immersed in the electrolyte (DO: 1.23 ± 0.08 mg L-1, pH: 6.98 ± 0.1) to obtain an adsorption–desorption equilibrium (-60–0 min) in the dark. The catalytic degradation stage (0–60 min) was started under visible light irradiation with a visible light intensity of 100 mW m−2. Then, 1.5 mL liquid sample was withdrawn every 20 and 10 min at the adsorption–desorption and catalytic degradation stages, respectively, and filtrated through a 0.22 μm membrane. The concentrations of CBZ and Cr(VI) were measured using high-performance liquid chromatography and diphenylcarbazide method, respectively (Text S5). The intermediates were identified using ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Text S6). All samples were filtrated through a 0.22 μm membrane, and centrifuged at 12,000 r min−1 for 15 min. The supernatant was used to measure the total organic carbon (TOC) using the standard non–purgeable organic carbon method.

The stability of the photocatalytic materials was evaluated via a cycling experiment, and the conditions were the same as those in the water purification experiment. The effects of light intensity, working area, and substrate concentration were investigated. In addition, quenching experiments were conducted by introducing an excessive amount of tertiary butanol (TBA), benzoquinone (C6H4O2), and ammonium oxalate.

The chemical bonds and functional groups of PANI and CQDs were detected by FTIR, and various functional groups were detected on their surfaces (Fig. S7, Text S7). According to the XRD patterns (Fig. 4 a), all the photocatalytic electrodes exhibited characteristic diffraction peaks at 27.4° (110), 36.1° (101), 41.2° (111), 43.9° (210), 54.5° (211), 56.4° (220), 62.7° (002), 69.7° (112), and 74.3° (320), indicating that the TNAs mainly consisted of rutile TiO2 (PDF # 21–1276). Weak typical Ti peaks (PDF # 44–1294) were also observed, attributed to the Ti foil substance. No significant peak shift or decrease in peak intensity was observed in the XRD patterns of TC and TP, indicating that the decoration of CQDs and PANI barely affected the lattice structure of the TNAs. A broad characteristic peak from 20° to 30° was regarded as the existence of PANI, exhibiting an amorphous characteristic [25]. However, no evident PANI diffraction peak was found in TP or TCP, which might have been due to the low PANI loading or strong TNAs intensity.

According to the survey spectra (Fig. 4b), the high-resolution spectra of these dominant elements in TNAs (Ti and O), TC (Ti, O, and C), TP (Ti, O, and N), and TCP (Ti, O, C, and N) were investigated. Two significant peaks were detected at 458.7 eV and 464.4 eV in the Ti 2p spectrum of TNAs (Fig. 4c), ascribing to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 peaks, respectively [26]. The binding energy separation between the two peak centres was 5.7 eV with a symmetric Ti 2p spectrum shape, indicating that Ti existed in four valence states in the TNAs [27,28]. However, these peaks exhibited a slight shift to 458.6 eV (Ti 2p3/2) and 464.3 eV (Ti 2p1/2) after the in-situ electro-polymerisation of aniline. This shift indicates that the introduction of PANI contributed to the reduction of the surface electron density of the rutile TiO2, resulting in the electrons on the TiO2 surface being easily transferred to the adjacent atom [29]. In contrast, compared to the TNAs, two identical peaks were deconvoluted from the Ti 2p spectrum of TC, revealing that the dipping process barely affected the lattice structure of the rutile TNAs. The XPS results of the C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s high-resolution spectra further confirmed the successful decoration of CQDs and PANI on the surface of the TNAs (Text S8).

The structure of the photocatalytic electrodes was crucial for the separation of electron-hole pairs. Therefore, based on the XPS results, the TCP structure was analysed (Fig. S8). MPA had carboxylate (–COOH) and thiol (-SH) functional groups [22]. Therefore, CQDs can easily connect with rutile TiO2 in the presence of MPA as a bridge. In addition, CQDs can be directly combined with PANI and TNAs owing to their abundant functional groups (including PANI-H···O-CQDs···O-TiO2, PANI-N···O-CQDs, PANI-N···H-hydrogen bond···O-CQDs, and PANI-H···O-CQDs). Furthermore, the lattice oxygen of TiO2 participated in the connection between the TNAs and PANI via hydrogen bonds (TiO2-O···H···N-PANI), forming a π-π delocalised conjugated structure (the p orbital of N atoms with the π delocalised conjugated benzenoid) during the in situ electro-polymerisation process [29,30]. Therefore, some CQDs were located between the TNAs and PANI as the mediator layer (TNAs-CQDs-PANI, Z-scheme structure). Other CQDs acted as a surface layer (TNAs-PANI-CQDs, type II structure). However, the TCP electrode was dominated by the TNAs-CQDs-PANI structure owing to the synthesis procedure.

The Raman spectra and N2 adsorption–desorption curves of the PVA-rGO cathode are shown in Fig. S9, revealing the successful synthesis of rGO and obtaining a relatively high absorption capacity (specific surface area: 187 m2 g−1) (Text S9). In addition, the specific surface areas of the prepared photoanodes are listed in Table S3. The specific surface areas were significantly increased after decorating CQDs or PANI owing to the larger specific surface areas of CQDs and PANI.

The light absorption capability of the photocatalytic electrode is crucial to the photocatalytic activity. TiO2 is well recognised as a photoresponse inorganic semiconductor material with an adsorption edge near the ultraviolet–visible transitional region [31]. TNAs exhibited a typical rutile TiO2 adsorption edge at 413 nm (Fig. 5 a). According to Eq. S1 (Text S10), the band gap of rutile TNAs was 3.00 eV, which is consistent with the theoretical band gap value [32]. After decorating CQDs and PANI, the adsorption edge was redshifted to 424 nm (2.92 eV) and 439 nm (2.82 eV), respectively. TP exhibited a light adsorption characteristic similar to that of TNAs. In contrast, the light adsorption intensity (>420 nm) was significantly increased in TC, resulting from the strong light adsorption capability of CQDs. Furthermore, TCP showed the strongest light adsorption capability among all photocatalytic electrodes, and the adsorption edge was shifted to 470 nm, corresponding to 2.64 eV. The significant increase in light absorption intensity was derived from the increase in PANI and CQDs loadings. The Tauc plot data from the UV–vis DRS were calculated using Eq. S2 (Text S10), the band gaps of TNAs, TC, TP, and TCP were 3.01 eV, 2.93 eV, 2.84 eV, and 2.61 eV, respectively (Fig. 5b), which were similar to the UV–vis DRS results.

To further confirm the energy band position, Mott–Schottky curves (Fig. 5c) and valence–band XPS (Fig. 5d) were measured. All Mott–Schottky curves exhibited positive slopes, representing n-type semiconductor characteristics [33]. It is well recognised that the bottom EVB value can be estimated using the Fermi level for n-type semiconductors [34]. The flat-band potentials of TNAs, TC, TP, and TCP were −0.23 eV, −0.32 eV, −1.78 eV, and −1.91 eV, respectively, calculated using Eq. S3 (Text S10). For an n-type semiconductor, the flat-band potential equals the Fermi level value. Therefore, the ECB value was near the flat-band potential. The EVB values were 2.79 eV, 2.63 eV, 1.04 eV, and 0.76 eV for TNAs, TC, TP, and TCP, respectively. Combined with the Mott–Schottky and valence-band XPS results, the band gap values of TNAs, TC, TP, and TCP were 3.02 eV, 2.95 eV, 2.82 eV, and 2.67 eV, respectively, further confirming the energy band alignment.

In order to evaluate the photoelectric conversion capability under visible-light excitation, the transient photocurrent densities of TNAs, TC, TP, and TCP during five on/off light cycles at steady state (bias of 0 VAg/AgCl) are shown in Fig. 6 a. After 5 cycles, the steady current densities of TCP, TP, and TC were approximately 0.885 mA/cm−2, 0.267 mA/cm−2, and 0.109 mA/cm−2, respectively, which were 18.8, 5.7, and 2.3, respectively, than that of pure TNAs (0.047 mA/cm−2). The increment of the TCP photocurrent density was higher than the summed increments of TC and TP, revealing the good synergetic effect of PANI and CQDs on enhancing the photoelectric conversion capability of TNAs. In addition, all photocatalytic electrodes exhibited relatively high transient photocurrent densities in the first cycle, resulting from the high separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs as soon as the visible light was turned on [35]. Then, all the photocurrent signals gradually decreased owing to the recombination of electron-hole pairs. After decreasing, stable states were rapidly achieved owing to the equilibrium between the electron diffusion rate and its generation rate.

To illustrate the bias effect on the photoelectric conversion capability, transient photocurrent densities with a bias in the range from −0.4 VAg/AgCl to 1.0 VAg/AgCl were recorded (Fig. 6b). TNAs and TC showed photocurrent density variation trends that were completely different from those of TP and TCP. The photocurrent densities of TC and TNAs sharply increased to the maximum values as soon as the light was turned on, reaching 0.404 mA/cm−2 and 0.256 mA/cm−2, respectively, at a bias of −0.1 VAg/AgCl, respectively. Then, the photocurrent densities gradually decreased with increasing bias, reaching 0.265 mA/cm−2 and 0.167 mA/cm−2 at a bias of 0.95 VAg/AgCl. The results indicated that the photoelectric conversion capabilities of the TNAs and TC were strongly affected by the bias. In contrast, the photocurrent densities of TP and TCP were significantly increased over the entire potential range from −0.4 VAg/AgCl to 1.0 VAg/AgCl, reaching up to 1.595 mA/cm−2 and 0.857 mA/cm−2, respectively, at a bias of 0.95 VAg/AgCl; these values were nearly 9.5 and 5.1 times than that of TNAs. The photocurrent densities were in the order of TNAs < TC < TP < TCP over the entire potential range. The results revealed that the photoelectric conversion capability of TNAs was enhanced after modification.

A series of EIS tests were conducted to investigate the photoelectrochemical properties of the photocatalytic electrodes. Prior to EIS measurement, stable EOCP values were achieved for all samples (Fig. S10). A larger EOCP shift indicates that more electrons were generated at the interface between the photoanode and electrolyte. The stable EOCP values were decreased under visible-light irradiation by 0.144 VAg/AgCl, 0.124 VAg/AgCl, 0.204 VAg/AgCl, and 0.438 VAg/AgCl for TNAs, TC, TP, and TCP, respectively. The results indicated TCP exhibited that best photoelectric conversion performance among all the prepared photocatalytic electrodes.

Typical Nyquist plots are shown in Fig. 7 . All the Nyquist curves exhibited incomplete arcs, revealing that electron transport was the dominant process at the interface between the photoanode and electrolyte [36]. All the impedance values were significantly decreased by over two orders of magnitude under visible-light excitation (Table S4), revealing that the electron transport process was enhanced. The impedance values were in the order of TNAs (137 Ω cm2) > TP (109 Ω cm2) > TC (93 Ω cm2) > TCP (22 Ω cm2), indicating that the decoration process enhanced the electron transport capability. In addition, according to the Bode plots (Fig. 7c and 7d), the break frequencies were decreased under visible-light excitation from 19.31 Hz to 3.73 Hz (TNAs), from 10 Hz to 2.68 Hz (TC), from 2.68 Hz to 1.93 Hz (TP), and from 1.79 Hz to 1.39 Hz (TCP), revealing the acceleration of the electron transport rate [37]. According to Eq. S4 (Text S10), the electron lifetime increased with the decrease in the break frequency. The rapid transport process could effectively inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs, which was consistent with the photocurrent density results. In addition, the maximum phase angle values decreased under visible irradiation (Fig. S11). Combined with the Nyquist results, the electron transport process was enhanced. Meanwhile, the peak centre of TPC was shifted to a lower frequency, indicating that the electron transport process was strong in TPC.

The PL spectra are widely applied to evaluate the separation efficiency of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs, and all PL spectra were obtained under an excitation wavelength of 300 nm (Fig. S12). It can be seen that all photoanodes exhibited broad peak range from 400 nm to 600 nm, revealing the recombination of the photo-generated electrons and holes [38,39]. In addition, the peak intensity was gradually decreased in the order: TNAs > TC > TP > TCP, indicating that TCP could efficiency suppress the recombination of the electrons and holes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the water purification performance
ce:section.ce:para: The water purification performance of the prepared electrode materials was evaluated in different reaction systems. The adsorption capabilities of all the photocatalytic electrodes are shown in Fig. S13, and the corresponding adsorption results are listed in Table S5. All the photocatalytic electrodes exhibited weak adsorption capabilities (less than 5%) for both CBZ and Cr(VI). However, the adsorption capability of TNAs was slightly improved after modification owing to the abundant functional groups on the CQDs and PANI surface.

The apparent adsorption (-60–0 min) and degradation/reduction (0–60 min) efficiencies were calculated (Table S4, kinetic constants in Table S5). In order to evaluate the actual degradation/reduction performances, the effect of the adsorption–desorption effect was removed (Table S6, kinetic constants in Table S7). The water purification performance of the photocatalytic electrodes in the photocatalytic system is shown in Fig. 8 . Compared to the TNAs (CBZ: 8.33%, Cr(VI): 2.64%), the water purification performance of TC and TCP was enhanced, reaching 14.44% (CBZ) and 4.07% (Cr(VI)) for TC and 44.67% (CBZ) and 11.94% (Cr(VI)) for TCP. However, the water purification performance decreased for TP (CBZ: 0.92%, Cr(VI): 3.94%%). The results were inconsistent with the photocatalytic activity test results (TCP > TP > TC > TNAs). In order to obtain a deep understanding of this problem, EPR spin trapping experiments were conducted to detect the species of radical groups under visible-light irradiation. Typical DMPO-OH peaks (hydroxyl radicals, •OH) in the prompt formation of a (1:2:2:1) quartet and weak DMPO-O2 peaks (superoxide radical, •O2 –) were obtained in TNAs, TC, and TCP (Fig. 9 ), while no significant peaks were observed in TP.

The active radical’s production process could be explained by analysing energy band structure. The CB (-0.23 VRHE) and VB (2.79 VRHE) positions of the TNAs were slightly lower and higher than those of O2/•O2 – (-0.13 VRHE) and H2O/•OH (2.38 VRHE), respectively, exhibiting a potential •O2 – and •OH production capability. However, the CB position was close to O2/•O2 –, resulting in a weak •O2 – production performance. Therefore, only strong •OH peaks were detected in the TNAs (Fig. 9a). Similarly, only •OH was detected in the TC (Fig. 9b). Based on the π-π* transition in the benzenoid and quinonoid units, PANI exhibited semiconductor characteristics. TP exhibited a typical type II heterojunction structure (Fig. 9c). Both the LUMO and HOMO positions of PANI were lower than the CB and VB positions of the TNAs, respectively. Therefore, the electrons and holes were populated at TNAsCB and PANIHOMO, respectively. The PANIHOMO position (0.62 VRHE) was lower than that of H2O/•OH, resulting in the oxidation of CBZ by holes at PANIHOMO, rather than the generation of •OH (Eqs. S5-S10, Text S10). In addition, the photogenerated electrons at TNAsCB were mainly used to reduce Cr(VI) (Eq. S10, Text S10), accompanied by weak secondary reactions, including the generation of •O2 –, producing H2O2, (Eqs. 12–14, Text S10). However, since the TNAsCB position (-0.23 VRHE) was slightly lower than that of O2/•O2 –, no characteristic •O2 – peaks were obtained from the EPR results. Therefore, CBZ degradation mainly relied on oxidation via the holes at PANIHOMO, resulting in poor CBZ degradation performance. However, the photocatalytic activity indicated a good photoelectric conversion capability of TP, exhibiting an acceptable Cr(VI) reduction performance.

For TCP, the photogenerated electrons were transferred from TNAsCB to PANIHOMO via CQDs as a bridge. Then, the electrons at PANILUMO (-2.14 VRHE) and holes at TNAsVB (2.79 VRHE) were used to reduce Cr(VI) and produce •OH, respectively. Cr(VI) was directly reduced by the electrons at PANILUMO as the dominant reaction, and O2/•O2 – and O2/H2O2 as secondary reactions consumed the electrons at PANILUMO. Moreover, the PANILUMO position was lower than that of TNAsCB, facilitating the generation of •O2 –. Therefore, the CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performances of TCP were the best among all the photocatalytic electrodes. The results indicated that the developed TCP photocatalytic electrode material significantly enhanced the water purification performance.

According to the EPR results, the CBZ degradation process mainly relied on the •OH and •O2 –. However, various secondary reactions coexist with the degradation process. Therefore, in order to identify the contribution of each process, a quenching experiment was conducted (Fig. S14). The CBZ degradation efficiencies of the control and control + TBA groups were 15.33% and 81.91%, respectively. Therefore, 66.58% of CBZ was removed by the •OH process. Similarly, 3.83% and 13.27% CBZ were removed via hole oxidation and the •O2 – process, respectively. In addition, only 0.99% CBZ was degraded via other processes, such as H2O2 and electron reduction. After normalisation processing, the contributions of •OH, •O2 –, holes, and other processes were 78.63%, 15.67%, 4.52%, and 1.18%, respectively. The results indicated that •OH oxidation was the dominant process in TCP.

The specific surface area of the electrode was far less than that of powdery materials, resulting in a weak water purification performance (TCP: CBZ, 44.67%; Cr(VI), 11.94%). In order to enhance the simultaneous CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performance of the photocatalytic electrode, a PVA-rGO cathode was added to the photocatalytic system to construct PEC systems. Compared to the photocatalytic electrodes, the adsorption capability of the PVA-rGO cathode was strong (33.68% for CBZ and 12.78% for Cr(VI); Figs. S15 and Table S5). Compared to the photocatalytic system, the actual CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performances were significantly improved in the single-reactor PEC system (Fig. S16; Table S8), reaching 35.03% (CBZ) and 4.23% (Cr(VI)) for TNAs, 48.36% (CBZ) and 9.16% (Cr(VI)) for TC, 3.65% (CBZ) and 11.16% (Cr(VI)) for TP, and 77.63% (CBZ) and 25.68% (Cr(VI)) for TCP. The results demonstrated that the single-reactor PEC system contributes to further exerting the electrode performance by strengthening the directional electron transfer capability and suppressing the recombination of the electron-hole pairs (pathway VI in Fig. 10 ). In addition, the increment of TCP was larger than that of the other photocatalytic electrodes.

In the single reactor system, the photoanode and cathode might interact when immersed in the same electrolyte. Therefore, the photoanode and cathode were separated and immersed in the different reactors, forming a two-reactor PEC system. Compared to the single reactor, the actual water purification performances were further improved in the two reactors-1 (Table S8, G9-G12, Fig. S17) and two reactors-2 (Table S8, G13-G16, Fig. S18), and the CBZ degradation performance was better in the two reactors-1 than the two reactors-2. The addition of Cr(VI) in the PEC reactor in two reactors-1, rather than two reactors-2, slightly enhanced the CBZ degradation performance. Cr(VI) could serve as the electron acceptor to react with the electrons on the TNAsCB or PANIHOMO (pathway V in Fig. 10), facilitating the generation of more holes. Therefore, the CBZ degradation efficiency was slightly higher (TNAs: 1.22%; TC: 2.50%; TCP: 2.35%) in two reactors-1 compared to two reactors-2. However, TP was slightly lower (TP: 0.62%) in two reactors-1 than two reactors-2, probably due to the different CBZ degradation mechanisms. CBZ degradation of TP mainly relied on hole oxidation, rather than the radical process. The oxidation process was weak and affected by the CBZ concentration, resulting in different results. In addition, considering CBZ degradation is related to the oxidation and catalysis processes, the CBZ degradation can be assumed using the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Eqs. S15-S17). The kapp of G12 was the highest (0.03101 min−1, Table S7; 0.02647 min−1, Table S9) among all groups. The results indicated that the two PEC reactor systems exhibited better water purification performance than the other systems, especially for the two reactors-1. Compared to the previous literatures, our TCP electrode exhibited a good simultaneous carbamazepine degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performances (Table S10).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of the stability, the key factors, and the degradation pathways
ce:section.ce:para: TCP exhibited the best water purification performance among all the prepared photocatalytic materials. Therefore, stability and key factor experiments were conducted using TCP. The mineralisation efficiency was lower in the photocatalytic system than in the PEC systems (Fig. 11 a). TOC was rapidly removed at the early stage and slightly decreased at the mid-late stage in the single reactor, which was mainly due to the good adsorption capability of the PVA-rGO cathode. In addition, the mineralisation capability of the single reactor (37.8%) was weaker than that of the two reactors-1 (52.56%) and two reactors-2 (49.39%), revealing that the two reactors contributed to the electrode performance. The TOC removal (Fig. 11b), CBZ degradation (Fig. 11c), and Cr(VI) reduction (Fig. 11d) efficiencies were relatively stable after five cycles in all reaction systems, and each cycle run was closely followed by the last run without any elution or desorption process. However, a relatively high efficiency was obtained in the first run, following by a sharp decrease in the following runs in the presence of rGO cathode. The strong adsorption performance only played a crucial role in the first run, and then, the photocatalytic activity dominated the following cycling runs in terms of the water purification performance. In addition, the pristine TCP and the TCP after cycle test were observed (Fig. S19). Compared to the pristine TCP, a relatively thick layer can still be observed on the surface of TNAs, revealing the good stability of TCP during the water purification experiment.

The effects of the key factors were evaluated, including light intensity, photoanode working area, and substrate concentration. Both CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction efficiencies gradually increased with increasing irradiation intensity (from 100 mW m−2 to 300 mW m−2, Fig. S20) and effective working area (from 1 cm2 to 4 cm2, Fig. S21). With increasing light intensity or working area, more energy is adsorbed and utilised by the photocatalytic material, accelerating the generation of electron-hole pairs and enhancing the water purification performance. According to the results, the working area exhibited a stronger effect on the water purification performance than light intensity. In addition, the substrate concentration was another key factor affecting the water purification performance. With the increase in the substrate concentration from 1times (CBZ: 10 μM; Cr(VI): 0.68 mM) to 3 times (CBZ: 30 μM; Cr(VI): 2.04 mM), both CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performance gradually decreased (Fig. S22), revealing that the substrate concentration was the limiting factor under the conditions of 100 mW m−2 light intensity and 1 cm2 working area.

A total of ten intermediates were detected during a 60 min CBZ degradation process in the different degradation systems using UPLC-MS in ESI + mode. Detailed information on the molecular structures, detention times, and m/z values are summarised in Table S11. Based on these intermediates and the previous literature, CBZ degradation pathways have been proposed (Fig. 12 ). It should be noted that not all intermediates were detected in all degradation systems. P7 and P10 were not detected in the photocatalytic system, P9 in the single reactor, P2, P6, and P10 in two reactors-1, P4, P5, and P10 in two reactors-2. However, these undetected intermediates did not affect the identification of the pathways. P1 (licarbazepine) was generated via the electrophilic attack of •OH at the olefin double bond of the central heterocyclic ring, and P3 (10-(1-aminovinyl)-10,10a-dihydroacridine-9-carbaldehyde) was formed through the attack of •OH at the double bond of CBZ. After the dehydration process, P1 was transformed into P2 (10,11-epoxycarbamazepine) and further transformed into P3 via deaminisation. Under the attack of •OH, P8 (aldehyde) was obtained from P3. Meanwhile, P4 (acridine-9-carboxaldehyde) was formed via the deaminisation of P3. P5 (acridine) was generated from P4 via a de-ketonisation process and further turned into P6 (acridone) in the presence of •OH. P6 was obtained from P8 via the attack of •OH and a ketonisation process. According to previous studies, P9 should be detected as an intermediate during the conversion process of P6 into P8, but it was not. P10 and P7 were produced via the ring cleavage process and the attack process of •OH of P6, respectively [40–45]. Eventually, CBZ was turned into CO2 and H2O via the attack of P1 and P10 by •OH.

In this study, a visible-light-driven Z-scheme TiO2-CQDs-PANI photocatalytic electrode was fabricated. The photocatalyst not only exhibited a strong visible-light adsorption capability, a narrowed band gap, and a high photocurrent density after introducing PANI and utilising CQDs as an electron transfer medium, but also showed good water purification performance. The CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performances were enhanced by nearly 38.34% and 9.30% after modification of TNAs, respectively. Moreover, the CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction performances were further enhanced by nearly32.96%–38.62% and 11.76%–13.74%, respectively, after constructing PEC systems. The radical pathway dominated by •OH (78.63%) played a vital role in the PEC system for CBZ degradation, and the radical pathway •O2 – (15.67%), a non-radical pathway (holes and others, 5.70%) played a subordinate role. The enhanced CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction processes were ascribed to the strong visible light adsorption, separation of the electron-hole pair, and photoelectric conversion capabilities. The dehydration, hydrolysis, deaminisation, de-ketonisation, and ring-opened reactions were found to be responsible for the decomposition and mineralisation of CBZ. Therefore, this work proposes a novel approach for the construction of a CQD-based Z-scheme photoanode for simultaneous CBZ degradation and Cr(VI) reduction in photocatalytic systems and different PEC systems for efficient water purification.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2019TQ0292) and National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFC1800703).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.127571.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
